content.text: State Space Models (SSMs) are a family of sequence models derived from classical control-theory state space representations. Unlike Transformers, which compute all-to-all attention over a sequence, SSMs propagate a compact hidden state forward in time — processing each token in constant memory. This makes them theoretically attractive for very long sequences where attention's quadratic complexity becomes prohibitive.

This sub-subtopic explains the mathematical foundations of SSMs: the continuous and discrete formulations, the convolution-recurrence duality that makes them trainable in parallel, the HiPPO initialisation scheme that gives S4 its long-range memory, and the limitations that motivate the selective mechanism in Mamba.

attrs.alt: SSM dual computation modes: recurrence for inference, convolution for training

content.text: The classical continuous-time SSM describes how a hidden state
evolves in response to an input signal
— the state transition matrix (how the state evolves over time)
— the input projection (how input affects state)
— the output projection (how state maps to output)
— skip connection from input to output (often set to zero in LLM use)
is the "memory" of the system — how much information it can carry forward in time. A larger
allows the model to remember more distant past, at the cost of a larger hidden state vector.
Real sequences are discrete (tokens, not continuous signals). To apply the continuous-time SSM to sequences, we discretise it using the
(ZOH) method with a learnable step size
is a learned parameter that controls how much the model "zooms in" on the time axis. A small
makes the model focus on fine-grained local patterns; a large
makes it skip over local detail and track long-range trends. In Mamba,
— this is the key selective mechanism.
is diagonal (in its latent space), reducing the matrix exponential to element-wise scalar exponentials:
The key insight that makes SSMs trainable in parallel is that, for
parameters (A, B, C, Δ fixed across all timesteps), the recurrence can be unrolled into a convolution:
This convolution can be computed in
using the FFT, making training fully parallel — just like attention. Inference, however, uses the recurrence, which processes each new token in
memory (the hidden state vector) rather than growing a KV cache.
The critical practical challenge for SSMs is initialising the state matrix
such that the model can actually represent long-range dependencies at the start of training. A random
typically forgets the distant past exponentially fast.
framework (High-order Polynomial Projection Operators, Gu et al. 2020) provides a principled initialisation for

derived from the theory of function approximation. The HiPPO matrix projects the history of the input onto a basis of orthogonal polynomials, preserving a compressed representation of all past tokens up to the current time.
The specific HiPPO-LegS (Legendre, scaled) matrix is:

attrs.latex: A_{nk} = -\begin{cases} (2n+1)^{1/2}(2k+1)^{1/2} & \text{if } n > k \\ n+1 & \text{if } n = k \\ 0 & \text{if } n < k \end{cases}

content.text: This structured initialisation means that at the start of training, the SSM already has the capacity to represent long-range patterns — rather than having to learn this from scratch. S4 (Gu et al. 2022) showed that using HiPPO initialisation and a diagonal-plus-low-rank approximation of
enables state dimensions of N = 64–256 to match or exceed Transformer performance on long-range benchmarks like PathX (16,384-length sequences).
S4 (Structured State Space for Sequences) makes two key contributions beyond the basic SSM formulation:
A is parameterised as A = Λ - PQᵀ where Λ is diagonal and P, Q are low-rank. This allows efficient computation of the matrix exponential and the kernel K̄ in O(N) rather than O(N³).
The DPLR structure is initialised to approximate the HiPPO matrix, giving the model long-range memory from the first step of training.
The combined effect is that S4 computes the full SSM kernel
in closed form using Cauchy kernels — a computation that costs
are derived from the DPLR decomposition. This Cauchy kernel can be evaluated at all
lags simultaneously using numerically stable algorithms.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class S4Layer(nn.Module):
    """
    Simplified S4 layer using diagonal state space model.
    Full S4 uses DPLR; this illustration uses pure diagonal for clarity.
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model, d_state=64, dt_min=0.001, dt_max=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.d_model = d_model
        self.d_state = d_state   # N

        # Diagonal A: parameterised as log-magnitude + phase for stability
        # Initialised near HiPPO spectrum (negative real part for stability)
        log_A_real = torch.log(0.5 * torch.ones(d_model, d_state))
        A_imag     = torch.pi * torch.arange(d_state).float().unsqueeze(0).expand(d_model, -1)
        self.log_A_real = nn.Parameter(log_A_real)
        self.A_imag     = nn.Parameter(A_imag)

        # B, C projections
        self.B = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(d_model, d_state) * 0.01)
        self.C = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(d_model, d_state) * 0.01)

        # Log step size (per channel)
        log_dt = torch.rand(d_model) * (torch.log(torch.tensor(dt_max))
                                       - torch.log(torch.tensor(dt_min)))
        log_dt += torch.log(torch.tensor(dt_min))
        self.log_dt = nn.Parameter(log_dt)

        # Skip connection
        self.D = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(d_model))

    def _compute_kernel(self, L):
        """Compute SSM convolutional kernel of length L."""
        dt = self.log_dt.exp()                        # [d_model]
        A  = -self.log_A_real.exp() + 1j * self.A_imag  # [d_model, d_state], complex

        # ZOH discretisation for diagonal A
        A_bar = torch.exp(dt.unsqueeze(-1) * A)       # [d_model, d_state]
        B_bar = (A_bar - 1) / A * self.B              # [d_model, d_state]

        # Unroll kernel: K_j = C A_bar^j B_bar
        j     = torch.arange(L, device=A.device).float()
        # Powers: A_bar^j via exp(j * log(A_bar))
        log_A = torch.log(A_bar)                       # [d_model, d_state]
        # K[j] = Re(C * exp(j * log_A_bar) * B_bar)
        powers = torch.exp(j.view(L, 1, 1) * log_A.unsqueeze(0))  # [L, d_model, d_state]
        K = (powers * (self.C * B_bar).unsqueeze(0)).sum(-1).real  # [L, d_model]
        return K.permute(1, 0)  # [d_model, L]

    def forward(self, x):
        """
        x: [B, L, d_model]
        Returns: [B, L, d_model]
        """
        B, L, d = x.shape
        K = self._compute_kernel(L)   # [d_model, L]

        # Causal convolution via FFT
        x_f = torch.fft.rfft(x.permute(0, 2, 1), n=2*L)   # [B, d_model, L+1]
        K_f = torch.fft.rfft(K, n=2*L)                      # [d_model, L+1]
        y   = torch.fft.irfft(x_f * K_f.unsqueeze(0), n=2*L)[..., :L]  # [B, d_model, L]

        return y.permute(0, 2, 1) + x * self.D.unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)
fixed across all positions in the sequence. This
is what makes the convolution computation valid (the kernel
is the same regardless of position). However, it also means the model cannot selectively focus on or ignore specific inputs based on their content.

Consider a retrieval task: given a long document followed by a question, the model should selectively compress the document while attending heavily to the question tokens. A linear SSM cannot do this — it applies the same kernel to every token regardless of content. In practice, linear SSMs under-perform attention on tasks requiring content-dependent selection.
Input-dependent (B, C, Δ vary per token)
Valid — enables O(T log T) training
Weak — uniform kernel over all inputs
This limitation motivates Mamba's selective state space model (S6): making

functions of the input at each position. The price is losing the convolution form — but Mamba recovers parallel training via a hardware-aware parallel scan (associative scan) that runs entirely in SRAM.
Numerical Stability and the Diagonal Approximation
is stable only if the eigenvalues of
have negative real parts — i.e., the system is stable in the control-theory sense. For HiPPO-LegS, this is guaranteed by construction. However, naively parameterising a general
matrix and exponentiating it is both expensive (
S4 and its successors use several strategies to keep training numerically stable:
The simplest approach is to restrict

. The diagonal assumption loses some expressive power compared to the full DPLR parameterisation, but empirically the gap is small and the computational savings are significant. Mamba uses purely diagonal A.
is typically parameterised in log-polar form with the real part constrained to be negative:
always, so the state decays over time rather than growing unboundedly.
Stabilising the kernel via log-domain accumulation
and small eigenvalues, floating-point underflow is a risk. The standard fix is to accumulate in log space:

def stable_kernel(log_A_bar, L):
    """
    Compute SSM kernel K_bar = [C A_bar^0 B_bar, ..., C A_bar^{L-1} B_bar]
    in numerically stable form using log-space accumulation.
    log_A_bar: [d_model, d_state] complex log of discretised A
    """
    # k-th element: exp(k * log_A_bar)
    k    = torch.arange(L, device=log_A_bar.device).float()    # [L]
    # [L, d_model, d_state] -> multiply each k by log_A_bar
    exps = torch.exp(k[:, None, None] * log_A_bar[None, :, :]) # [L, d_model, d_state]
    return exps   # caller multiplies by C and B_bar and sums over d_state
Linear (time-invariant) SSMs can be trained with FFT convolution. But once B, C, and
become input-dependent (as in Mamba), the SSM kernel changes at every position — the convolution trick is invalid.
(also called the associative scan or prefix scan). The recurrence:
can be written as a sequence of linear maps composed together. Composition of linear maps is associative:

attrs.latex: (f_b \circ f_a)(h) = \bar{A}_b(\bar{A}_a h + \bar{B}_a x_a) + \bar{B}_b x_b = (\bar{A}_b \bar{A}_a) h + (\bar{A}_b \bar{B}_a x_a + \bar{B}_b x_b)

content.text: This means we can represent each step as a pair
and combine them via the associative operation:

attrs.latex: (m_b, v_b) \cdot (m_a, v_a) = (m_b m_a,\; m_b v_a + v_b)

content.text: A prefix scan over these pairs computes all outputs
parallel steps using a binary tree reduction, with each node performing one associative combine. This is the

def associative_scan(A_bar, B_bar_x):
    """
    Parallel prefix scan for SSM.
    A_bar:   [B, L, d_model, d_state]  -- discretised state transitions
    B_bar_x: [B, L, d_model, d_state]  -- B_bar * x_t at each position

    Returns h: [B, L, d_model, d_state]  -- all hidden states
    """
    B, L, d, N = A_bar.shape

    # Each element is a (M, V) pair: M = A_bar_t, V = B_bar_x_t
    # Combine: (M_b, V_b) * (M_a, V_a) = (M_b * M_a, M_b * V_a + V_b)

    # Use a simple sequential scan here; real implementations use
    # torch.associative_scan (PyTorch 2.x) or a custom CUDA kernel.
    h = torch.zeros(B, d, N, device=A_bar.device, dtype=A_bar.dtype)
    outputs = []
    for t in range(L):
        h = A_bar[:, t] * h + B_bar_x[:, t]   # [B, d, N]
        outputs.append(h)

    return torch.stack(outputs, dim=1)  # [B, L, d, N]

# PyTorch 2.x native associative scan:
# from torch._higher_order_ops import associative_scan
# def combine(a, b):
#     return (b[0] * a[0], b[0] * a[1] + b[1])
# h = associative_scan(combine, (A_bar, B_bar_x), dim=1)

Mamba's actual implementation fuses the parallel scan into a single CUDA kernel that keeps the entire hidden state in SRAM throughout the scan, avoiding the HBM round-trips that would otherwise dominate memory bandwidth. This is the key reason Mamba achieves near-linear scaling in practice despite the O(TN) theoretical cost.

(LRA, Tay et al. 2021) is the standard benchmark suite for comparing sequence models on tasks that require integrating information over thousands of positions. Six tasks test different aspects of long-range dependency:

S4 substantially outperforms vanilla Transformers on all LRA tasks and is the only model to successfully complete Path-X (16,384 pixels) without running out of memory. The Transformer fails Path-X because quadratic attention over 16,384 tokens exceeds typical GPU memory limits at practical batch sizes.

However, LRA tests a specific regime — tasks with very long sequences but simple content. On standard NLP benchmarks (GLUE, SuperGLUE) and language modelling (WikiText-103, The Pile), linear SSMs significantly lag behind Transformers. This is the content-based selection problem: linear SSMs cannot skip uninformative tokens, so they perform poorly on tasks where a small fraction of positions carry the relevant signal.
Practical Usage: SSM Layers in PyTorch
The mamba-ssm library provides production-quality SSM implementations. Installing and using Mamba (a selective SSM) in practice:

from mamba_ssm import Mamba

# Single Mamba layer (selective SSM replacing one FFN+attention block)
layer = Mamba(
    d_model=512,      # model dimension
    d_state=16,       # SSM state dimension N (hidden state size)
    d_conv=4,         # local convolution width (short-range features)
    expand=2,         # inner dimension = d_model * expand
).cuda()

# Forward pass — identical API to a Transformer layer
x   = torch.randn(2, 1024, 512).cuda()  # [batch, seq_len, d_model]
out = layer(x)                           # [batch, seq_len, d_model]
print(out.shape)  # torch.Size([2, 1024, 512])

# Autoregressive step (O(N) per token — no growing KV cache)
from mamba_ssm.utils.generation import InferenceParams
params = InferenceParams(max_seqlen=2048, max_batch_size=1)
token  = torch.randn(1, 1, 512).cuda()
out    = layer(token, inference_params=params)  # updates internal state in-place
For a full Mamba language model, Mamba stacks
such blocks with layer norms and residual connections — identical in structure to a Transformer but with SSM layers instead of attention + FFN:
h'(t) = Ah(t) + Bx(t), y(t) = Ch(t) — maps sequence input to output via a latent state
ZOH with step Δ converts ODE to recurrence: h_t = Āh_{t-1} + B̄x_t, y_t = Ch_t
time-invariant SSMs can be computed as a single convolution K̄ ⊛ x in O(T log T) during training, then switched to O(N) recurrence at inference
principled initialisation of A from polynomial approximation theory; gives S4 long-range memory without training from random
diagonal-plus-low-rank A enables efficient kernel computation via Cauchy kernels in O(N + T)
time-invariant parameters cannot perform content-based selection — all tokens are processed with the same kernel regardless of their values
make B, C, Δ input-dependent; overcome linear SSM weaknesses at the cost of losing the FFT convolution training speedup
